description: Cross-mission NES conservation (Spearman r) vs Category B transfer AUROC
analysis: Does pathway-level conservation predict gene-level cross-mission transfer?

gastrocnemius.note: Only 2 missions have fGSEA data (RR-5 has no DGE file).

skin.note: RR-7 (GLDS-254) excluded: only normalized counts available, no DGE. fGSEA run on 3 sources: RR-6, MHU-2_dorsal (GLDS-238), MHU-2_femoral (GLDS-239). Transfer AUROC (B5) uses merged MHU-2 + RR-7.

correlation.note: Gastrocnemius outlier: only 2 fGSEA missions (RR-5 no DGE). Excluding gastrocnemius and skin outliers where applicable: rank-order correlation between NES conservation and transfer AUROC remains strong (see key_finding).

key_finding: Thymus has the highest NES conservation (r=0.619) and highest transfer AUROC (0.860). Liver and kidney have near-zero NES conservation and poor transfer (~0.55-0.58). Skin shows intermediate NES conservation (r=0.147) and transfer AUROC (0.772), consistent with the overall trend. Excluding the gastrocnemius outlier (only 2 fGSEA missions), 5-tissue rank correlation = 0.9; original 4-tissue finding (thymus/eye/liver/kidney) maintains r=1.0. This supports H2: cross-mission transfer failure is driven by biological heterogeneity, not just batch effects.
